role_summary: First African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction (1994-2000); defense counsel for the Friendship 9 in 1961 and again at their 2015 exoneration; founding chair of the South Carolina Legislative Black Caucus (1973-75).
biographical_paragraph: In December 1994, Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. became the first African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since the end of Reconstruction, holding the post until his retirement on March 23, 2000, his sixty-ninth birthday [src: 2]. He had reached the court along the long civil rights apprenticeship of a Southern Black lawyer: a 1954 law degree at South Carolina State College, election to the Third Judicial Circuit bench in 1976, and an associate-justice seat on the state supreme court from 1985 [src: 2]. His most cited civil-rights case came earlier, in 1961, when he defended the Friendship 9, the Black junior-college students arrested for trying to desegregate the McCrory's lunch counter in Rock Hill, South Carolina; fifty-four years later, in 2015, he represented the surviving members of that group when a state court vacated the original convictions [src: 2]. Finney was born March 23, 1931 in Smithfield, Virginia, took his Bachelor's at Claflin College in 1952, and as a state legislator from 1972 chaired the House Judiciary Committee (the first African-American to do so in modern South Carolina) and was the charter chairperson of the Legislative Black Caucus from 1973 to 1975 [src: 2]. His daughter Nikky Finney is a poet at the University of South Carolina [src: 2]. He died of complications of Alzheimer's disease on December 3, 2017 in Columbia, South Carolina [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 39 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
